unitTitle: KS2 Y4 Repetition in games (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Using loops to create shapes
lessons.objectives:
I can list an everyday job as steps that repeat
I can read a loop in Scratch and predict what it will draw
I can change a loop to draw a different shape
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on the board: repeat · loop · count-controlled loop · predict · code · block. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; Scratch open on the same starter project. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — things that repeat. Look at the clock image. Pupils SORT everyday jobs into ‘repeats’ / ‘happens once’ on the starter worksheet. Idea: a loop means ‘do it again’.
2. (10–20) I-do — name the parts of the Scratch screen. Use the screen image: Code area · Blocks · Stage · green flag · Sprite. Pupils LABEL the screen on the activity worksheet.
3. (20–35) We-do — read the repeat block. Show ‘repeat 4 → move 50, turn 90’. Count the sides: 4 = a square. PREDICT what ‘repeat 3’ would draw. Likely error: thinking the number is the size → fix-words “the number is how many times, not how big.”
4. (35–50) You-do — change the loop. Pupils change the repeat number and the turn to draw a new shape, then screenshot it on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: choose the shape a loop draws.  • Core: match each repeat number to its shape.  • Challenge: work out the turn for a triangle.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil shows one shape. TA: prompt, do not do it for them. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Different loops
lessons.objectives:
I can tell a forever loop from a count-controlled loop
I can change a loop to make a given outcome
I can choose the right loop for a job
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: forever loop · infinite loop · count-controlled loop · repeat · at the same time. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; Scratch open. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — two kinds of loop. Show the forever block and the repeat block side by side. A forever loop never stops; a count-controlled loop stops after a number. Pupils MATCH each loop to what it does on the starter worksheet.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — when to use each. ‘Flap wings forever’ = forever loop. ‘Draw 4 sides’ = repeat 4. Pupils SORT jobs into ‘forever’ / ‘count a number’ on the activity worksheet. Likely error: using forever when you want it to stop → fix-words “if it must stop, count it.”
3. (20–40) You-do — change a loop. Pupils swap a forever loop for repeat 10 (or back) and watch the change, then screenshot it.
   • Support: choose which loop never stops.  • Core: fill the repeat number for a given job.  • Challenge: explain why a flashing star uses a forever loop.
4. (40–50) Two things at once — two sprites can each run their own loop at the same time. Show it; name it.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Animate your name
lessons.objectives:
I can choose an action to repeat for each letter
I can say what the repeated action should do
I can say how well my animation worked
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: animation · costume · sprite · loop · at the same time · evaluate. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; a name project with letter sprites ready in Scratch. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — what is an animation? Show the animated name. An animation changes the look again and again — a loop. Pupils CHOOSE what an animation does on the starter worksheet.
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — repeat an action on each letter. Use the Looks blocks (next costume / change colour) inside a forever loop. Every letter sprite runs its own loop, all started by the green flag, so they animate at the same time.
3. (20–30) Design — pupils plan their name on the activity worksheet: ORDER the steps to build one letter, then choose the repeated action per letter.
4. (30–48) You-do — build and run. Pupils add a loop to each letter, click the green flag, then screenshot the running animation.
   • Support: choose the block that repeats.  • Core: match each action to its outcome.  • Challenge: explain why all letters move together.
5. (48–55) Evaluate — rate how well your animation worked and write one change. Self-tick the three “I can…”. Likely error: forgetting the loop so it runs once → fix-words “no loop, it happens once.” No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Modifying a game
lessons.objectives:
I can find the parts of a loop I can change
I can say what my change does
I can re-use code on a new sprite
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: game · sprite · forever loop · modify (change) · re-use. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the shark game open in Scratch. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — match the game to its design. Show the shark game. Pupils MATCH each sprite to what it does on the starter worksheet (the shark swims forever; the fish moves).
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do — find what can change in a loop. Inside a forever loop you can change the number, the block, or the sprite. Change ‘change x by 10’ to ‘change x by 5’ → it moves slower. Pupils SORT changes into ‘makes it faster’ / ‘makes it slower’ on the activity worksheet. Likely error: deleting the forever block → fix-words “keep the loop, change what is inside.”
3. (20–35) You-do — make a change. Pupils change one number inside the loop and screenshot the before and after.
4. (35–48) Re-use code on a new sprite. Add a new sprite and copy the loop onto it so it moves too.
   • Support: choose what a smaller number does.  • Core: fill the effect of a change.  • Challenge: explain why re-using code saves time.
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil shows one change they made. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Shark game on the stage (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Designing a game
lessons.objectives:
I can say where repetition is used in a game
I can pick parts of a model game to use in my own
I can write a simple design for my game
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: design · algorithm · model project · repetition · sprite · plan. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet and the design (activity) worksheet; the model bat game open in Scratch. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — find the repetition. Play the model bat game. Pupils CHOOSE which parts repeat on the starter worksheet (the bats keep flapping; they keep moving down).
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — what makes a good plan. A design says: the sprites, what each one does, and which action repeats. ORDER the design steps on the design worksheet.
3. (20–45) You-do — design your own game. Pupils fill the design worksheet: name the sprites, draw/describe the stage, and write the algorithm for one sprite (with a loop). Screenshot or photo the plan.
   • Support: choose which action will repeat.  • Core: fill the algorithm for one sprite.  • Challenge: explain what your game will do and why the loop is needed.
4. (45–52) Share — swap with a partner; say one thing that is clear and one to add. Update the design.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”. Reassure: you build this next lesson. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Model bat game (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Creating our games
lessons.objectives:
I can follow my design to build my game
I can find and fix a mistake in my code
I can say how well my game works
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on the board: build · algorithm · loop · fix (debug) · test · evaluate. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet, their design from last lesson, and the evaluation (activity) worksheet; Scratch open. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — read your design. Pupils ORDER the build steps on the starter worksheet (open project → add sprites → add the loops → run → fix → save).
2. (8–15) I-do → we-do — follow the algorithm. Build one sprite from a design together, including its loop. Click green flag and test. Likely error: code runs once, no loop → fix-words “add the loop to repeat it.”
3. (15–45) You-do — build your game. Pupils follow their own design, add the loops, test, and fix. Calm independent work. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: choose what to do when the sprite does not move.  • Core: fill what you changed to fix a bug.  • Challenge: explain one improvement you made.
4. (45–52) Test and evaluate — screenshot the finished game; rate how well it works and write one thing that went well and one to improve on the evaluation worksheet.
5. (52–55) Showcase — each pupil shows their game; self-tick the three “I can…”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.
